narrative: I'm sorry to share this news, but the law school's admission model predicted a 47% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which was enough to lead to the rejection of your application. Your profile includes a Year 1 school decile rank of 3, a Year 3 decile rank of 2, lower-middle family income, an LSAT score of 20.7, an undergraduate GPA of 2.4, being male, Asian background, and planning part-time work status. Compared to typical students in the dataset—who average around 5.7 for Year 1 deciles, 5.5 for Year 3, much higher LSAT scores near 36.7, GPAs around 3.2, and mostly full-time enrollment with White backgrounds—your academics stand out as notably lower, which caught the model's attention. The strongest factor driving this prediction was your Year 3 decile rank, as it significantly pushed the model toward expecting bar failure since it's well below average. Next in influence was your LSAT score, which being so much lower than typical also strongly nudged the prediction toward failure. Your Year 1 decile rank followed closely, reinforcing that concern with its below-average performance. Family income in the lower-middle bracket had a notable but slightly lesser push in the same direction, as did your Asian racial background relative to the mostly White dataset. Your undergraduate GPA, part-time work plans, and male gender rounded out the influences, all contributing toward the failure prediction to lesser degrees. In essence, no single feature pulled strongly in favor of passing; everything aligned to highlight risks based on your specifics versus the stronger profiles that typically pass. To improve your future chances, focus on bolstering academic performance through targeted study habits or prep courses, as your decile ranks suggest room for stronger grades ahead. Retaking the LSAT to aim closer to average scores could make a big difference, since it was a top influencer. Finally, considering full-time enrollment might help, as part-time status underrepresented strong bar passers in the data. With these realistic steps, you could shift the model's view in your favor next time. Keep pushing—you've got potential to turn this around.
